title: Commercial Photography

alternatives.name: Commercial and industrial commissioned-imagery firms
inclusion_criteria: Firm is classified in NAICS 541922 and a plurality of its photography revenue comes from commercial or industrial users rather than private consumers.
Core deliverables are commissioned still or short-form moving images for products, properties, facilities, advertising, corporate communications, or industrial documentation.
Operating model may be a fixed studio, an on-location production team, or a managed field-photographer network, provided commissioned commercial and industrial imaging is the principal service line.
exclusion_criteria: Portrait, school, passport, wedding, or private-event photography is the principal revenue source.
Convention and meeting coverage is the principal revenue source.

The firm is principally a motion-picture producer, photofinisher, stock-image licensor, equipment lessor, or independent artistic or news photographer rather than a commissioned commercial-photography operator.
alternatives.rationale: The reproducible base allocation is 6 normalized target-band firms multiplied by the 2022 Economic Census share of NAICS 541922 revenue reported as commercial and industrial photography services (85.6%), yielding 5.136 and rounded to 5.14. The same table reports that 99.5% of industry establishments offer this product, while operator sources show materially different studio, field-network, and managed-production structures inside the label. Because product incidence and industrywide revenue share do not identify the principal model of the six target-band firms, the range is deliberately wide.

alternatives.name: Convention and meeting photography specialists
inclusion_criteria: Firm is classified in NAICS 541922 and a plurality of photography revenue comes from conventions, conferences, trade shows, or corporate meetings.
Recurring workflow centers on on-site event coverage, rapid image delivery, exhibitor or attendee services, and multi-photographer event staffing.
exclusion_criteria: Private weddings and social events are the principal revenue source.
Product, property, advertising, industrial, or other commissioned commercial imagery outside meetings and conventions is the principal revenue source.
alternatives.rationale: The base allocation is 6 multiplied by the 2022 Economic Census convention and meeting photography revenue share of 1.9%, yielding 0.114 and rounded to 0.11. The product appears at 15.7% of NAICS 541922 establishments and nationwide specialist operators exist, so a revenue-share allocation can materially undercount target-band specialists; the upper endpoint preserves that classification risk.

alternatives.name: Portrait and private-event crossover firms
inclusion_criteria: Firm is reported in NAICS 541922 but a plurality of photography revenue comes from portraits, weddings, or other private special events.
Consumer session, event, print, or digital-package sales are the dominant workflow even if the firm also serves business clients.
exclusion_criteria: Commercial and industrial commissioned imagery is the principal revenue source.
Convention and meeting coverage is the principal revenue source.
alternatives.rationale: The base allocation is 6 multiplied by the combined 2022 Economic Census NAICS 541922 revenue shares for portrait photography (5.0%) and wedding and special-event photography (2.0%), yielding 0.42. These products overlap the conceptual boundary with NAICS 541921 and occur at many establishments, so the range allows for both crossover and classification error among the six target-band firms.

residual.name: Mixed, licensing-led, rental-led, and otherwise unclassified firms
residual.rationale: This is the remaining 5.5% of the six-firm base after allocating 85.6% to commercial and industrial commissioned imagery, 1.9% to convention and meeting specialists, and 7.0% to portrait and private-event crossover firms. It retains firms with no classifiable plurality or whose principal economics are licensing, equipment rental, photofinishing, or other reported products.

sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau Statistics of U.S. Businesses
2022 Economic Census EC2200NAPCSINDPRD: Industry by Products for the U.S. and States
2022 NAICS 541922 Commercial Photography definition and cross-references
Professional Images convention and corporate-event photography services

target_archetype.selection_basis: Start with the normalized v4.1 estimate of 6 firms in the $1–10M EBITDA band. Allocate base firm-equivalents mechanically using mutually exclusive 2022 Economic Census product-revenue shares: 85.6% commercial and industrial commissioned imagery = 5.14; 1.9% convention and meeting photography = 0.11; 5.0% portrait plus 2.0% wedding and special-event photography = 0.42; remaining 5.5% = 0.33 residual. The largest base count is therefore commercial-industrial-commissioned. This is a proxy allocation, not observed target-band firm classification: product lines may coexist within establishments, industrywide receipt weights may differ sharply from the six target-band firms, and the normalized n_band estimate itself is LOW quality. The two largest alternatives by base count are 5.14 and 0.42, they are not within 10% of each other, and their supported ranges of 2.4–6.0 and 0.0–2.3 do not overlap, so selection_uncertainty is false.

value_basis.scope: Cash labor and independent-contractor cost structure of the selected commercial and industrial commissioned-imagery archetype, widened to cover plausible reclassification into convention/meeting, portrait/private-event, studio, and managed field-network operating models.

labor_contractor_cash_cost_share.rationale: No source located directly measures cash labor plus contractor cost as a share of total cash costs for target-band firms in the selected archetype. The normalized dataset's 0.3098 broad-NAICS labor share excludes a target-specific contractor bridge and cannot be used deterministically. The class-neutral bounded assumption is therefore intentionally broad enough to cover employee-heavy studios and contractor-heavy field or event networks.
caveats: This is a frozen bounded assumption, not empirical evidence, and requires downstream readiness UNPROVEN.
The range is not a margin, payroll-to-revenue ratio, or broad-NAICS substitution; it is the assumed share of total cash costs paid to labor and contractors.
Selection uncertainty is reflected in the wide range; no narrower target-specific endpoint is supported.

roles.title: Photographers, videographers, assistants, and subcontract capture crews
roles.rationale: Frozen mutually exclusive assumption for personnel and contractor cash costs incurred in image capture and on-site crew work.

roles.title: Retouching, editing, color, quality control, and digital asset delivery
roles.rationale: Frozen mutually exclusive assumption for post-capture processing, quality control, and delivery labor or contractors.

roles.title: Production coordination, scheduling, account, and client service
roles.rationale: Frozen mutually exclusive assumption for project coordination and client-facing operating work not included in capture or postproduction.

roles.title: Styling, set, equipment, and technical production support
roles.rationale: Frozen mutually exclusive assumption for specialized production-support labor and contractors, including stylists and technical crew.

roles.title: Sales, management, finance, and general administration
roles.rationale: Frozen mutually exclusive assumption for remaining commercial leadership and administrative cash labor cost.

value_basis.rationale: The 5-digit OEWS role mix in the normalized dataset spans both portrait and commercial photography and omits a defensible contractor-cost bridge. Treating it as target-specific would violate the v4.1 value-basis rule. This specification therefore freezes a transparent assumption basis with no evidentiary source claim, broad functional roles that remain meaningful across the overlapping archetypes, and weights summing exactly to one.
caveats: Do not replace these roles with the normalized dataset's 5-digit occupation shares.
Do not attach source claims to the assumption or improve its evidence quality during the run.
Honor the exact five-role membership and weights; missing or noncomparable workflow evidence remains missing rather than changing the basis.
The primary record describes only commercial and industrial commissioned-imagery firms, not all of NAICS 541922.

source_hints.source: Direct target-firm workflow observations, time-and-motion records, job-cost records, and vendor deployment documentation
source_hints.why: Needed to map intervention timing and realized task coverage to the frozen five-role cash-cost basis without substituting broad occupation data.

source_hints.source: Target-firm contracts, renewal cohorts, bid documents, rate cards, and customer interviews segmented by studio, field-network, and event workflow
source_hints.why: Needed to test whether any cost change is retained by the operator rather than passed through to clients or contractors.

source_hints.source: Closed-business transaction databases, broker records, and ownership registries filtered to the selected archetype and EBITDA band
source_hints.why: Needed to establish a count and range of plausibly purchasable operators rather than using all NAICS firms.

source_hints.source: Closed target-company deal documents and lender or quality-of-earnings materials with entry and downside-exit multiples
source_hints.why: Needed to support target-archetype valuation inputs and scenario endpoints.

source_hints.source: Longitudinal business-register or credit-file cohorts for commissioned commercial-photography operators of comparable size and model
source_hints.why: Needed to support year-five operator survival with an explicit population and horizon bridge.

implementation: For each frozen role, which specific workflows are affected, what share of that role's cash cost is addressable, and what direct evidence supports each base and endpoint?
What implementation schedule, adoption friction, review burden, rework, integration cost, and ongoing operating cost apply over five years for target-band commissioned-imagery firms?
How do supported implementation ranges differ among fixed studios, managed field networks, and convention or event operators without changing the selected record?
commercial_retention: What contract terms, bidding behavior, client concentration, turnaround commitments, and contractor pass-through mechanisms determine retained savings for the selected archetype?
What direct or explicitly bridged evidence supports the five-year commercial-retention base and endpoints?
sale_availability: How many independent U.S. firms meeting the selected archetype and $1–10M EBITDA definition were offered or sold in a defined recent period?
How should platform, agency, photofinishing, portrait, and mixed-model listings be excluded, and what uncertainty remains from undisclosed transactions?
valuation_robustness: What closed-deal evidence supports entry and downside-exit multiples for target-band commissioned commercial-photography operators?
How sensitive are supported multiples to contractor dependence, customer concentration, owner production work, equipment needs, and mixed service revenue?
operator_survival: What evidence supports five-year survival for firms matching the selected archetype, size band, and ownership model?
How are mergers, trade-name changes, platform conversion, and owner exit distinguished from operating failure?

special_notes: Archetype selection was completed before any research into AI economics, pricing capture, implementation outcomes, or valuation outcomes.
The normalized target-band estimate is only six firms and has LOW quality; product-share allocation yields reproducible base counts but does not observe the business model of those six firms.
Base archetype counts sum to 5.67 and cover 94.5% of the six-firm estimate; the 0.33 residual completes the base total to 6.00.

The two largest base-count archetypes are 5.14 and 0.42, their supported ranges of 2.4–6.0 and 0.0–2.3 do not overlap, and selection_uncertainty is false; downstream readiness remains UNPROVEN because the normalized target-band count is LOW quality and the value basis is an ASSUMPTION with NONE quality.
The selected archetype contains studio, on-location, and managed-network variants; downstream research must preserve the single record and represent supported consequences through ranges.
The value basis is an ASSUMPTION with NONE quality because the available broad-NAICS labor share and 5-digit role mix cannot support target-specific employee-plus-contractor weights.
No claim in this specification should be interpreted as an NAICS-wide economic score or outcome.
